title: LEOPARD: Lightweight Learned Guidance for Equality Saturation in Compiler Optimization
description: We propose LEOPARD, a compiler optimization system that addresses the phase-ordering problem by combining equality saturation with a lightweight learned model to guide e-graph construction. Unlike prior work (Aurora) that uses expensive RL with GNN/RNN for query optimization, LEOPARD employs lightweight supervised learning (small MLP or gradient-boosted trees) for fast inference, making it practical for production compilers. The system includes graceful degradation mechanisms to handle prediction uncertainty.
motivation: The phase-ordering problem has plagued compilers for decades. Traditional approaches apply optimizations sequentially, where poor ordering leads to suboptimal code. Equality saturation (ES) offers a principled solution by representing multiple equivalent programs simultaneously using e-graphs, avoiding destructive rewriting. However, ES suffers from memory explosion as the e-graph grows exponentially. Recent work (Aurora, 2024) demonstrated learned guidance using RL with GNN/RNN, but this is computationally expensive and domain-specific. There is no system that provides lightweight, fast learned guidance for general-purpose compiler optimization that can be deployed in production.
proposed_approach: LEOPARD consists of four components: (1) A lightweight learned rule scorer that predicts the optimization potential of applying specific rewrite rules in specific contexts, using a small MLP or gradient-boosted trees for fast inference (<1ms per decision). (2) An adaptive e-graph construction algorithm that applies rules in order of predicted benefit until memory budgets are reached, periodically re-scoring based on e-graph evolution. (3) Graceful degradation mechanisms that fall back to heuristics or exhaustive search when prediction confidence is low, ensuring correctness and avoiding catastrophic failures. (4) A learned extraction component that selects the optimal program from the final e-graph using a cost model trained to predict actual runtime performance. The system is trained via supervised learning on data collected from running ES with random rule selection, learning to predict eventual improvements from immediate contexts.
key_innovations: First system to use lightweight supervised learning (vs. expensive RL) for learned rule selection in equality saturation for general-purpose compiler optimization
Explicit focus on inference speed and production deployment (vs. training-time exploration quality)
Graceful degradation mechanisms that ensure correctness when predictions are uncertain
Direct comparison and differentiation from Aurora (2024), demonstrating that simple models can achieve comparable results to GNN+RNN RL at much lower inference cost

related_work.title: egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation
related_work.authors: Max Willsey, Chandrakana Nandi, Yisu Remy Wang, Oliver Flatt, Zachary Tatlock, Pavel Panchekha
related_work.key_contribution: Developed the egg library for equality saturation, enabling practical ES applications
related_work.difference: LEOPARD builds on egg but adds learned guidance to address memory explosion; egg uses exhaustive or bounded rule application

related_work.title: Learned Graph Rewriting with Equality Saturation: A New Paradigm in Relational Query Rewrite and Beyond
related_work.authors: George-Octavian Barbulescu, Taiyi Wang, Zak Singh, Eiko Yoneki
related_work.key_contribution: Demonstrated learned guidance for ES using RL with GNN+RNN for SQL query optimization
related_work.difference: Aurora uses expensive RL (200K+ steps) with GNN+RNN for query optimization; LEOPARD uses lightweight supervised learning (small MLP/GBDT) for general compiler optimization, prioritizing inference speed for production deployment

related_work.title: Optimizing Tensor Computation Graphs with Equality Saturation and Monte Carlo Tree Search
related_work.authors: Jakob Hartmann, Guoliang He, Eiko Yoneki
related_work.key_contribution: Used MCTS to guide e-graph construction for tensor programs, avoiding phase-ordering during construction
related_work.difference: MCTS requires expensive per-program search with many rollouts; LEOPARD learns transferable patterns that predict rule benefits without per-program search

related_work.title: Guided Equality Saturation
related_work.key_contribution: Proposed sketch-guided ES where programmers provide intermediate goals as 'sketches' to manage e-graph size
related_work.difference: Requires manual programmer guidance through sketches; LEOPARD learns guidance automatically from data

related_work.title: AutoPhase: Compiler Phase-Ordering for High Level Synthesis with Deep Reinforcement Learning
related_work.key_contribution: Uses RL to find good pass sequences for compilers, treating the compiler as an environment
related_work.difference: Treats compiler as black box and searches for pass sequences; LEOPARD uses ES to avoid phase-ordering fundamentally and learns to guide the ES process itself

related_work.title: Large Language Models for Compiler Optimization
related_work.key_contribution: Trained LLMs to predict LLVM pass sequences and optimize code size
related_work.difference: LLMs require massive training data and lack correctness guarantees; LEOPARD uses a lightweight model with ES to maintain correctness

related_work.title: Fast and Optimal Extraction for Sparse Equality Graphs
related_work.authors: Amir Kafshdar Goharshady, Chun Kit Lam, Lionel Parreaux
related_work.key_contribution: Developed efficient algorithms for e-graph extraction based on treewidth
related_work.difference: Focuses only on extraction phase; LEOPARD addresses both construction guidance and extraction

hypothesis: A lightweight supervised learning model (small MLP or gradient-boosted trees) trained to predict the optimization potential of rewrite rules can guide e-graph construction to achieve within 80% of exhaustive equality saturation's code quality while using 40-60% less memory, with minimal inference overhead suitable for production compiler deployment.

confirming: LEOPARD achieves ≥80% of the speedup of exhaustive equality saturation with ≤60% of the memory usage
The learned scorer achieves >65% accuracy in predicting beneficial rules (above random baseline)
LEOPARD generalizes to unseen programs with <15% performance degradation compared to training on those programs
Inference overhead is <5% of total compilation time
System degrades gracefully when predictions are uncertain (never worse than random selection)
refuting: Rule selection patterns are not transferable across programs (scorer accuracy <55% on test set)
Memory constraints fundamentally prevent finding high-quality solutions regardless of guidance
The overhead of scoring outweighs benefits for realistic program sizes
The system fails to degrade gracefully when predictions are poor (performs worse than random)

components: Instrumentation of egg library for data collection
Implementation of lightweight rule scorer (MLP or GBDT)
feasibility.resource_requirements: No GPU required; all components are algorithmic/analytical

potential_impact: If successful, LEOPARD would enable practical deployment of equality saturation in production compilers by demonstrating that lightweight learned guidance (supervised learning with small models) can achieve comparable results to expensive RL-based approaches at a fraction of the inference cost. By including graceful degradation mechanisms, the system provides a pathway for cautious adoption in production environments where correctness guarantees are paramount.

differentiation_from_aurora.leopard_learning: Supervised learning with small MLP or gradient-boosted trees

poor_predictions.mitigation: Confidence threshold triggers fallback to exhaustive search

distribution_shift.cause: Test programs structurally different from training

local_optima.cause: Greedy rule selection misses better sequences
